content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 11, 2017

Kyle W. Holmes
Schools Legal Service
P.O. Box 2445
Bakersfield, CA 93303

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-200

Dear Mr. Holmes:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Board members of the Tehachapi Unified School District (the "District") regarding Government Code Section 1090 et seq.¹ Please note that we are only providing advice regarding Section 1090. Nothing in this letter should be considered advice on any statutes on the California Education Code, court rules, or administrative hearing practices. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

Finally, in regard to advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Kern County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Is a payment made by the District pursuant to a mandate of the California Department of Education ("CDE") or in compliance with an order of an administrative law judge a contract implicating Section 1090?

CONCLUSION

No. The payments are mandated by law and not based on negotiation, mutual consent, and do not involve mutual consideration like a contract. Making the payments under the circumstances you describe do not implicate Section 1090.

FACTS

Dean and Brenda Markham are legally married and reside together in the same household. Beginning in 2015 the Markhams had disagreements over the special education programming offered

¹ All further statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-200
Page No. 2

to their dependent minor child by the District, which culminated in the Markhams filing a request
for due process hearing with the office of administrative hearings pursuant to the Individuals with
Disabilities Education Act of 2004 ("IDEA") (20 U.S.C. § 1415 & Cal. Ed. Code § 56505) on
March 25, 2015.² On December 1, 2016 the Administrative Law Judge, after conducting an
Administrative Due Process Hearing, the ALJ ruled against the District and required the District to
reimburse Ms. Markham for her costs incurred in obtaining a private reading program provided by
the Lindamood-Bell company. The District appealed the ALJ's decision to the federal district court
for the Eastern District of California, which is still pending.

After that ALJ Decision was issued, Mr. Markham was sworn in as a newly elected Trustee
of the Board of Trustees of the Tehachapi Unified School District.

Approximately 90 days after the ALJ Decision was issued, the California Department of
Education ("CDE") initiated a compliance review of the District. You stated that the compliance
review is standard practice after an administrative finding of non-compliance with the IDEA and its
implementing regulations. On October 9, 2017, you provided supplemental information. You stated
that CDE has directed the District to make the reimbursement payment, plus interest through the
date of the payment, to the Parents of the student directly. You noted that CDE has also initiated a
"sanctions" hearing process slated to begin at the end of this month.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies.³ (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v.
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) When an officer with a
proscribed financial interest is a member of the governing body of a public entity, the prohibition of
section 1090 also extends to the entire body. (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. at 50.) The prohibition applies
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson, supra,
at pp. 646-649.)

We generally employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a
disqualifying conflict of interest under Section 1090.

• Step One: Is the official subject to the provisions of Section 1090?

² You also noted that the Markhams filed other actions that are not pertinent to this request.

File No. A-17-200
Page No. 3

• Step Two: Does the decision at issue involve a contract?

• Step Three: Is the official making or participating in making a contract?

• Step Four: Does the official have a financial interest in the contract?

• Step Five: Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply?

• Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply?

Your question specifically concerns step 2: Is the official making or participating in making a contract. Section 1090 applies only to a decision that involves a contract. To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 (1995).) For example, the Attorney General has set forth the mode of analysis for this question in the context of development agreements:

"'A contract is an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing.' (Civ. Code, § 1549.) 'It is essential to a contract that there should be: 1. Parties capable of contracting; 2. Their consent; 3. A lawful object; and, 4. A sufficient cause or consideration." (Civ.Code, § 1550.) A development agreement contemplates that both the city or county and the developer will agree to do or not to do certain things. Both parties will mutually consent to terms and conditions allowable under the law. Both will receive consideration. The developer will essentially receive the local agency's assurance that he can complete the project. The local agency in turn will reap the benefit of the development, with all the conditions it might legitimately require, such as streets, parks, and other public improvements or facilities." (78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230.)

You cite to 91 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 1 in your letter. However, that opinion is factually distinguishable from your facts. In addition, the opinion went to great lengths to keep their conclusion narrow.

"Resolution of this question requires us to consider again the issue whether the law recognizes an exception to Government Code section 1090 when the contract being examined concerns resolution of litigation... current jurisprudence construing and applying section 1090 compels us to advise that a compromise settlement of even a damage action such as the one presented here is vulnerable to invalidation because of the plaintiff council member's financial interest in the settlement.

* * *

"As a general rule, an agreement to settle a lawsuit is treated as a contract. [Citations omitted.] Courts have also characterized stipulated

File No. A-17-200
Page No. 4

judgments or consent judgments as having the attributes of contracts. [Citations
omitted.]" [Emphasis added.]

Similarly, Santa Clara Valley Water District v. Gross (1988) 200 Cal.App.3d 1363 (the case
the attorney general's opinion found "instructive") distinguished actions in the context of litigation
from contractual agreements. The case concerned a condemnation proceeding by a water district.
The landowner was a member of the board of directors of the condemning water district, and both
parties believed they were precluded by Section 1090 from engaging in any negotiations. At the end
of the proceeding, the trial court entered a postjudgment order denying the landowner litigation
expenses for failure to comply with Code Civ. Proc., § 1250.410 (requiring that the property owner
must file a final demand for compensation and the condemnor must file a final offer as a
prerequisite to recovery of litigation expenses).

The court concluded that the litigants were wrong in their assumption that Section 1090
applied at all to the court proceeding and that the belief that Section 1090 applied did not excuse the
litigants from their failure to comply with the prerequisite for recovery of expenses set forth in
Section 1250.410. The Court stated:

"Government Code section 1090 ... has no force in the context of a
condemnation action where the sale of property is accomplished by operation of
law and each side is ordinarily represented by counsel.

* * *

"It is understandable that the result in Thomson could have a chilling
effect on a landowner contemplating the sale of his property to a governmental
agency of which he is an elected member. Nevertheless, we find that the Thomson
case is not applicable here where the parties have availed themselves of court of
law to effect a sale by means of condemnation. In such a case, the rules of
procedure which apply to all litigants apply to them as well.

* * *

"In our view compliance with section 1250.410 is no more likely to create
an appearance of impropriety than participation in mandatory settlement
conferences. 'The Legislature [in § 1250.410] did not direct the parties to
'apprise' each other or 'communicate' with each other about an offer or demand."
(City of San Leandro v. Highsmith, supra, 123 Cal.App.3d 146, 155.) Rather it
directed that each file with the court, and serve upon the other, a formal offer and
demand, as an absolute prerequisite to an award of attorney's fees. This procedure
is not the equivalent of negotiations between the parties and consequently does
not run afoul of section 1090."

In contrast to a settlement agreement, making a payment in response to a CDF sanction
order or making a payment pursuant to ALJ ordered payments does not constitute a contract for
purposes of Section 1090. These payments are not based on negotiation, mutual consent, and do not

File No. A-17-200
Page No. 5

involve mutual consideration. Thus, making the payments under the circumstances you describe do
not implicate Section 1090.

Section 87100

You have not asked about, nor were you apparently authorized to ask about, Board Member
Markham's duties under Section 87100. We assume the Board Member will not be making,
participating in making, or influencing in his official capacity the Board's decisions whether to
make a payment to himself.⁴ If the Board Member has questions about his duties under Section
87100, he should contact us for advice.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

_________________________
⁴ Note that the consequence of a conflict of interest under Section 87100 is required disqualification.

QUESTION: Is a payment made by the District pursuant to a mandate of the California Department of 
Education ("COE") or in compliance with an order of an administrative law judge a contract 
implicating Section I 090?

CONCLUSION: No. The payments are mandated by law and not based on negotiation, mutual consent, and 
do not involve mutual consideration like a contract. Making the payments under the circumstances 
you describe do not implicate Section 1090.
